(originally launched as **Artsworld**) is a British [free-to-air](/wiki/Free-to-air "Free-to-air") television channel offering 24 hours a day of programmes dedicated to [highbrow arts](/wiki/Highbrow "Highbrow"), including theatrical performances, films, documentaries and music (such as opera performances and classical and jazz sessions). The channel is available in the United Kingdom through [Freeview](/wiki/Freeview_(UK) "Freeview (UK)"), [Freesat](/wiki/Freesat "Freesat"), [BT TV](/wiki/BT_TV "BT TV"), [Sky](/wiki/Sky_UK "Sky UK"), [Virgin Media](/wiki/Virgin_Media "Virgin Media"), and [TalkTalk TV](/wiki/TalkTalk_TV "TalkTalk TV") and in the Republic of Ireland via [Sky Ireland](/wiki/Sky_Ireland "Sky Ireland"), [Virgin Media Ireland](/wiki/Virgin_Media_Ireland "Virgin Media Ireland"), [Vodafone Ireland](/wiki/Vodafone_Ireland "Vodafone Ireland") and [Eir](/wiki/Eir_(telecommunications) "Eir (telecommunications)"), included in most basic subscription packs, but started life as a premium service requiring an additional payment on top of the monthly Sky subscription.[[2]](#cite_note-2) The channel launched on Freeview and Freesat as a free-to-air service in September 2020.

**Sky Arts** (originally launched as **Artsworld**) is a British [free-to-air](/wiki/Free-to-air "Free-to-air") television channel offering 24 hours a day of programmes dedicated to [highbrow arts](/wiki/Highbrow "Highbrow"), including theatrical performances, films, documentaries and music (such as opera performances and classical and jazz sessions). The channel is available in the United Kingdom through [Freeview](/wiki/Freeview_(UK) "Freeview (UK)"), [Freesat](/wiki/Freesat "Freesat"), [BT TV](/wiki/BT_TV "BT TV"), [Sky](/wiki/Sky_UK "Sky UK"), [Virgin Media](/wiki/Virgin_Media "Virgin Media"), and [TalkTalk TV](/wiki/TalkTalk_TV "TalkTalk TV") and in the Republic of Ireland via [Sky Ireland](/wiki/Sky_Ireland "Sky Ireland"), [Virgin Media Ireland](/wiki/Virgin_Media_Ireland "Virgin Media Ireland"), [Vodafone Ireland](/wiki/Vodafone_Ireland "Vodafone Ireland") and [Eir](/wiki/Eir_(telecommunications) "Eir (telecommunications)"), included in most basic subscription packs, but started life as a premium service requiring an additional payment on top of the monthly Sky subscription.[[2]](#cite_note-2) The channel launched on Freeview and Freesat as a free-to-air service in September 2020.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sky_Arts&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Artsworld (2000–07)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sky_Arts&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Artsworld (2000–07)")]

In its early days, it was owned and managed by a public partnership (Artsworld Channels) including [Sir Jeremy Isaacs](/wiki/Jeremy_Isaacs "Jeremy Isaacs"). However, the channel suffered severe financial difficulty. In July 2002, it even staged its own farewell party, only to find emergency funding that very evening. In 2003, with a skeleton staff, it was facing closure. At this point, [Sky](/wiki/Sky_Group "Sky Group") stepped in, taking an initial 50% stake.[[3]](#cite_note-guardian-3)

Sky subsequently bought out the remaining shareholders (including Isaacs) and in June 2005 took full control, reducing the staff further, and dropping the channel's premium subscription fee shortly afterwards.[[4]](#cite_note-4) 60 hours of classic music along with seven full-length operas were broadcast each month to help bring in potential new subscribers.
John Cassy, the channel manager of Artsworld, said: "It is great news for the arts that a dedicated cultural channel will be available to millions of households."[[3]](#cite_note-guardian-3)

### Sky Arts (2007–present)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sky_Arts&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Sky Arts (2007–present)")]

On 1 March 2007, Artsworld became Sky Arts and Artsworld HD became Sky Arts HD.[[5]](#cite_note-5) This resulted in all of BSkyB's wholly owned channels carrying the Sky name (until [Pick TV](/wiki/Pick_(TV_channel) "Pick (TV channel)") was launched and [Virgin Media Television](/wiki/Living_TV_Group "Living TV Group") – which included [Challenge](/wiki/Challenge_(TV_channel) "Challenge (TV channel)") – was acquired).

From 8 June 2007, Sky Arts introduced a series called *Friday Night Hijack*. Artists were invited to schedule a night of television that reflects their tastes, interests and passions. Guests included legendary punk DJ [Don Letts](/wiki/Don_Letts "Don Letts"), [Don McCullin](/wiki/Don_McCullin "Don McCullin"), [Saffron Burrows](/wiki/Saffron_Burrows "Saffron Burrows"), [Anthony Horowitz](/wiki/Anthony_Horowitz "Anthony Horowitz"), [Malcolm McLaren](/wiki/Malcolm_McLaren "Malcolm McLaren"), [Phill Jupitus](/wiki/Phill_Jupitus "Phill Jupitus"), [Germaine Greer](/wiki/Germaine_Greer "Germaine Greer"), [George Melly](/wiki/George_Melly "George Melly") and *[Reggie Perrin](/wiki/Reggie_Perrin "Reggie Perrin")* writer [David Nobbs](/wiki/David_Nobbs "David Nobbs").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Picks included a [Franz Ferdinand](/wiki/Franz_Ferdinand_(band) "Franz Ferdinand (band)") documentary entitled *Rock it to Rio*, a concert performance by [Damon Albarn](/wiki/Damon_Albarn "Damon Albarn")'s new band [The Good, the Bad & the Queen](/wiki/The_Good,_the_Bad_%26_the_Queen "The Good, the Bad & the Queen") as well as documentaries and films on [Salvador Dalí](/wiki/Salvador_Dal%C3%AD "Salvador Dalí"), [Andy Warhol](/wiki/Andy_Warhol "Andy Warhol"), [Rachel Whiteread](/wiki/Rachel_Whiteread "Rachel Whiteread") and [Elvis Costello](/wiki/Elvis_Costello "Elvis Costello"). This weekly feature was later moved and renamed *Sunday Night Hijack*.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In 2013, Sky Arts launched *[Portrait Artist of the Year](/wiki/Portrait_Artist_of_the_Year "Portrait Artist of the Year")*, a painting competition series presented by [Frank Skinner](/wiki/Frank_Skinner "Frank Skinner") and [Joan Bakewell](/wiki/Joan_Bakewell "Joan Bakewell"). The series is judged by art experts [Tai-Shan Schierenberg](/wiki/Tai-Shan_Schierenberg "Tai-Shan Schierenberg"), [Kathleen Soriano](/wiki/Kathleen_Soriano "Kathleen Soriano") and [Kate Bryan](/wiki/Kate_Bryan "Kate Bryan"). Nick Lord won the first series and was awarded a commission to paint [Hilary Mantel](/wiki/Hilary_Mantel "Hilary Mantel") for the [British Library](/wiki/British_Library "British Library").[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7) In 2014 the second series was won by Christian Hook and his portrait of [Alan Cumming](/wiki/Alan_Cumming "Alan Cumming") is now part of the [Scottish National Portrait Gallery](/wiki/Scottish_National_Portrait_Gallery "Scottish National Portrait Gallery") collection.[[8]](#cite_note-8) In 2015 Sky Arts introduced a development of the competition for landscape painters called *Landscape Artist of the Year* in association with [National Trust](/wiki/National_Trust_for_Places_of_Historic_Interest_or_Natural_Beauty "National Trust for Places of Historic Interest or Natural Beauty").[[9]](#cite_note-9) The first series was won by Nerine McIntyre and she was awarded a commission to paint the scene made famous by [John Constable](/wiki/John_Constable "John Constable") at [Flatford](/wiki/Flatford_Mill "Flatford Mill").

On 9 June 2015, Sky Arts 2 closed and merged its content with Sky Arts 1 to form one channel.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

### The free-to-air era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sky_Arts&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: The free-to-air era")]

On 17 September 2020, Sky Arts launched on [Freeview](/wiki/Freeview_(UK) "Freeview (UK)") and [Freesat](/wiki/Freesat "Freesat") as a free service.[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-ftalaunch-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14)

Since becoming a free-to-air service, the channel has picked up a number of acclaimed music documentaries such as *[King Rocker - A Film About Robert Lloyd and the Nightingales](/wiki/King_Rocker "King Rocker")*,[[15]](#cite_note-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17)[[18]](#cite_note-18)[[19]](#cite_note-19) *[Poly Styrene: I Am a Cliché](/wiki/Poly_Styrene:_I_Am_a_Clich%C3%A9 "Poly Styrene: I Am a Cliché")*[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22)[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[24]](#cite_note-24)[[25]](#cite_note-25)[[26]](#cite_note-26) and *Blitzed: The 80s Blitz Kids' Story*,[[27]](#cite_note-27) many of which were broadcast on Sky Arts a short while after premiering to the press.

As of 2024, series on Sky Arts[[28]](#cite_note-28)[[29]](#cite_note-29) include *Classic Albums*,[[30]](#cite_note-30) *The Great Songwriters*[[31]](#cite_note-31) and *Guy Garvey: From the Vaults*,[[32]](#cite_note-32)[[33]](#cite_note-33) with the channel also having picked up repeats of *Alfred Hitchcock Presents*,[[34]](#cite_note-34) *Tales of the Unexpected*[[35]](#cite_note-35) and *The Joy of Painting with Bob Ross*.[[36]](#cite_note-36) for its daily schedule.[[37]](#cite_note-37) In addition, episodes of the *[Grand Ole Opry](/wiki/Grand_Ole_Opry "Grand Ole Opry")*,[[38]](#cite_note-38) the long-running American [country music](/wiki/Country_music "Country music") programme,[[39]](#cite_note-Circle_Ends-39) and albums documentary series *Record On*[[40]](#cite_note-40)[[41]](#cite_note-41) have turned up in the schedules on an ad hoc basis, with the *Record On* episode concerning The Specials, *A Message to You*,[[42]](#cite_note-42) being scheduled as a one-off programme in a night of music shows,[[43]](#cite_note-43) alongside documentaries about The Clash[[44]](#cite_note-44) and [X-Ray Spex](/wiki/X-Ray_Spex "X-Ray Spex").[[45]](#cite_note-45)

## Channels

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sky_Arts&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Channels")]

### Sky Arts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sky_Arts&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Sky Arts")]

Sky Arts (formerly known as *Artsworld* and *Sky Arts 1*) focused on the more modern and independent side of Sky Arts' programming. Schedules included cutting-edge documentaries, cult films, and rock concerts.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] but since 9 June 2015 it has also featured the high brow programmes from the former Sky Arts 2.

### Sky Arts 2

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sky_Arts&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Sky Arts 2")]

Sky Arts +1 began broadcasting on 18 August 2008, filling the [Performance Channel](/wiki/Performance_Channel "Performance Channel")'s EPG slot (purchased by Sky) until the full launch of Sky Arts 2 on 20 October 2008.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] This channel focused on high brow programme and featured classical music, opera, dance, fine arts programming and reruns of *[Tales of the Unexpected](/wiki/Tales_of_the_Unexpected_(TV_series) "Tales of the Unexpected (TV series)")*.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

From 30 March to 14 April 2013, Sky Arts 2 was temporarily rebranded as Sky Arts Rieu in honour of [André Rieu](/wiki/Andr%C3%A9_Rieu "André Rieu"), broadcasting back-to-back concerts by the violinist.[[46]](#cite_note-46)

On 9 June 2015, at 06:00 [BST](/wiki/British_Summer_Time "British Summer Time"), Sky Arts 2 closed after showing its final classical music programme followed by promos for the new merged channel.

### Sky Arts HD

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sky_Arts&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Sky Arts HD")]

Artsworld HD was one of the launch channels on [Sky HD](/wiki/Sky%2B_HD "Sky+ HD") in 2006.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] It was a high-definition simulcast of Artsworld, and later Sky Arts, showing HD programmes when available, and upscaling standard-definition programmes.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Following the launch of Sky Arts 2, Sky Arts HD showed a mix of programmes in high definition from both channels – generally Sky Arts 1 all day on Mondays, Thursdays and Sundays and Sky Arts 2 until 7 pm on Tuesdays, Wednesdays, Fridays and Saturdays, switching to Sky Arts 1 from 7 pm onwards on those days.

On 2 March 2009, Sky Arts HD was split into two channels. Sky Arts 1 HD then broadcast from 7 pm to 2 am daily, and Sky Arts 2 HD from 8 am to 7 pm. The standard definition channels continued to broadcast a full schedule from 8 am to 2 am. Sky Arts 1 HD also broadcast a further hour of HD-only programming called 'Aquariavision' intended to be recorded by Sky+ HD owners.

As of June 2009, both Sky Arts 1 and 2 HD broadcast full-time between 6 am and 2 am.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

From 1 March 2010, all Sky Arts channels are broadcasting 24 hours a day.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

An on and off-air brand refresh for all four channels (Sky Arts 1, Sky Arts 1 HD (a simulcast), Sky Arts 2 and Sky Arts 2 HD (a simulcast)) was launched on 16 August 2010.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

On 9 June 2015, at 6 am, Sky Arts 2 HD closed down.

As of September 2020, Sky Arts is currently made up of the following channels: Sky Arts and Sky Arts HD (a simulcast).

### Sky Arts Ireland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sky_Arts&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Sky Arts Ireland")]

On 8 April 2021, Sky began broadcasting a version Sky Arts with adverts tailored to Ireland.[[47]](#cite_note-47)[[48]](#cite_note-48) The channel is registered with the broadcasting regulator in Luxembourg[[49]](#cite_note-49) with the [Autorité Luxembourgeoise Indépendante de l'Audiovisuel (ALIA)](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Autorit%C3%A9_luxembourgeoise_ind%C3%A9pendante_de_l%27audiovisuel "fr:Autorité luxembourgeoise indépendante de l'audiovisuel") (Independent Luxembourg Audiovisual Authority) making use of the classification system set by Ireland's [BAI](/wiki/Broadcasting_Authority_of_Ireland "Broadcasting Authority of Ireland") Code of Program Standards.

Sky Arts Ireland would later be closed down on 30 October 2025, with Irish viewers now receiving the UK version as before.[[50]](#cite_note-50)
